elements.sent-2411-001: This work explores the relationship between state space methods and Koopman operator-based methods for predicting the time-evolution of nonlinear dynamical systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: We demonstrate that extended dynamic mode decomposition with dictionary learning (EDMD-DL), when combined with a state space projection, is equivalent to a neural network representation of the nonlinear discrete-time flow map on the state space.
elements.sent-2411-003: We highlight how this projection step introduces nonlinearity into the evolution equations, enabling significantly improved EDMD-DL predictions.
elements.sent-2411-004: With this projection, EDMD-DL leads to a nonlinear dynamical system on the state space, which can be represented in either discrete or continuous time.
elements.sent-2411-005: This system has a natural structure for neural networks, where the state is first expanded into a high dimensional feature space followed by a linear mapping which represents the discrete-time map or the vector field as a linear combination of these features.
elements.sent-2411-006: Inspired by these observations, we implement several variations of neural ordinary differential equations (ODEs) and EDMD-DL, developed by combining different aspects of their respective model structures and training procedures.
elements.sent-2411-007: We evaluate these methods using numerical experiments on chaotic dynamics in the Lorenz system and a nine-mode model of turbulent shear flow, showing comparable performance across methods in terms of short-time trajectory prediction, reconstruction of long-time statistics, and prediction of rare events.
elements.sent-2411-008: We also show that these methods provide comparable performance to a non-Markovian approach in terms of prediction of extreme events.
elements.sent-2411-009: Accurately predicting the behavior of complex dynamical systems is a critical challenge in many scientific and engineering fields, especially with the increasing availability of large datasets.
elements.sent-2411-010: Two promising approaches for developing predictive models from time-series data are neural ODEs, which construct continuous-time models directly on the space of the original data, and approaches based on Koopman operator theory, which lift the data to a high dimensional space where the dynamics can be represented linearly.
elements.sent-2411-011: Recent works have shown that the predictive performance of Koopman-based methods can be significantly improved by projecting the prediction back to the state space on each timestep.
elements.sent-2411-012: We show that this step converts the linear dynamics on the space of observables to a nonlinear system on the state space, rendering the method essentially equivalent to a neural ODE of a particular form.
elements.sent-2411-013: This insight helps to form new connections between these distinct approaches and inspires new methods that combine the strengths of each.
elements.sent-2411-014: We validate these findings through numerical experiments and performance comparisons on two chaotic systems arising in fluid dynamics, showing that methods arising from each approach exhibit excellent, and nearly equivalent predictive performance.
elements.sent-2411-015: Developing methods for forecasting the time-evolution of complex systems governed by ordinary differential equations (ODEs) using models derived directly from data is of great importance to many scientific and engineering applications, especially as data from many of these systems has become more readily available in recent years.
elements.sent-2411-016: This demand has driven the development of many neural network architectures tailored to time-series data.
elements.sent-2411-017: Some of the most popular and effective modeling techniques for time sequence data include neural networks with recurrent architectures, such as long short-term memory networks (LSTM) \cite{hochreiter1997long or gated recurrent units (GRU) \cite{Cho2014 ; reservoir computing methods \cite{jaeger2004harnessing ; and transformer-based architectures \cite{vaswani2017attention (see, for example, Refs.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, these approaches implicitly abandon the dynamical systems point of view by constructing models which predict future time evolution from long sequences of past states.
elements.sent-2411-019: That is, these models are non-Markovian and thus effectively operate on a space of much higher dimension than the original state space.
elements.sent-2411-020: Here, our focus is on developing models using data obtained from systems which are known to be governed by differential equations.
elements.sent-2411-021: Therefore, we restrict ourselves to Markovian models, in which future time evolution is uniquely determined from the present state.
elements.sent-2411-022: In this work we consider parallels between neural network-based methods for modeling dynamical systems which represent the dynamics directly on the state space and those which represent the dynamics in the space of observable functions using Koopman operator approximations, as illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:explanatory .}
elements.sent-2411-023: In recent years, data-driven modeling techniques derived from Koopman operator theory have gained significant research attention due to their potential to provide linear representations for complex, nonlinear dynamical systems \cite{budisic_applied_2012,mezic_analysis_2013,brunton_modern_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: In the Koopman formalism, time evolution is considered in the space of observable functions of the states, rather than in the state space of the system.
elements.sent-2411-025: Therefore, though the time evolution is determined by a linear operator, this operator typically acts on an infinite dimensional function space.
elements.sent-2411-026: Much of the work in this area has focused on constructing useful finite-dimensional approximations of the Koopman operator directly from data.
elements.sent-2411-027: Such approximations hold great promise for many application areas, as they enable the use of modal analysis to identify structure in nonlinear systems \cite{mezic_spectral_2005,mezic_analysis_2013 , they can allow for model reduction when dealing with high-dimensional data \cite{rowley_model_2017 , and they can potentially be paired with linear methods for forecasting, estimation, and control \cite{otto_koopman_2021 .}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/Explanatory_EDMD_NODE.pdf} \caption{Schematic illustrating how EDMD-DL with a projection to the state space directly leads to a neural network representation of the nonlinear dynamics on the state space.} \label{fig:explanatory}
elements.sent-2411-028: Many numerical techniques have been developed for constructing finite-dimensional approximations of the Koopman operator.
elements.sent-2411-029: One of the earliest and most common approaches is dynamic mode decomposition (DMD), originally proposed by Schmid \cite{Schmid2010 , which determines a best-fit linear time-evolution operator (or its eigenvalues and eigenvectors) given time series data from the system (pairs of snapshots separated by one timestep).}
elements.sent-2411-030: DMD is appealing to many practitioners due to its simplicity and its ability to extract physically meaningful features or modes directly from data without any a priori knowledge of the system or dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-031: The connection between DMD and the Koopman operator was made by Rowley et al. \cite{Rowley2009 , which built upon the earlier works of Mezic that proposed a Koopman mode decomposition directly from data \cite{Mezic2004,mezic_spectral_2005 .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: As pointed out by Rowley, et al \cite{Rowley2009 , the finite dimensional approximation obtained from DMD can only be rigorously connected to the Koopman operator if the observable functions used in the computation lie within an invariant subspace spanned by eigenfunctions of the Koopman operator.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In practice, however, this is usually not the case, such as when the data is obtained directly from experimental observations.
elements.sent-2411-034: This lack of an invariant subspace then leads to closure issues with the numerically obtained matrix approximation of the operator, as the true operator propagates the selected observables forward in time into functions that cannot be represented as a linear combination of these observables.
elements.sent-2411-035: This leads to inaccurate approximations of Koopman modes and rapid error buildup when attempting to use the operator approximation for forecasting \cite{brunton_koopman_2016, Wu2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: These issues have led to numerous extensions of the original DMD algorithm \cite{Colbrook2023 , with many of them aiming to improve the function space on which the operator is approximated by a careful selection of observables.}
elements.sent-2411-037: One of the most prominent variations is extended dynamic mode decomposition (EDMD) \cite{williams_datadriven_2015 , which performs the DMD computation for the best-fit linear time evolution operator on measurement data that is ``lifted'' to a higher dimensional space by evaluating a pre-defined set of dictionary functions on the data.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Williams et al. \cite{williams_datadriven_2015 showed that EDMD offers an improvement upon DMD, as it converges to the same results that would be obtained from a Galerkin method in the limit of a large data set.}
elements.sent-2411-039: However, aside from some heuristic guidelines, the optimal choice of dictionary functions remained an open problem.
elements.sent-2411-040: In light of this challenge, many works have proposed methods to learn or discover an appropriate choice of observables to be used in EDMD.
elements.sent-2411-041: Li et al. \cite{li_extended_2017 proposed a method called EDMD with dictionary learning (EDMD-DL) which optimizes the dictionary used with EDMD by representing the dictionary as a neural network and optimizing its parameters by stochastic gradient descent to minimize a prediction loss.}
elements.sent-2411-042: They showed that this method yielded great improvements over standard choices of dictionary, both in terms of approximating Koopman eigenfunctions and eigenvalues and in forecasting capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-043: Recently, this method was extended by Constante-Amores, et al. \cite{constante-amores_enhancing_2024 , who showed that the optimization of the dictionary and the operator can be combined into a streamlined training procedure by backpropagating through the EDMD pseudoinverse calculation.}
elements.sent-2411-044: EDMD-DL typically allows for linear reconstruction of the state from the dictionary by explicitly including the state in the dictionary as non-trainable elements.
elements.sent-2411-045: Other works \cite{lusch_deep_2018, otto_linearly_2019 have proposed methods in which Koopman operators are approximated by learning linear dynamics on the latent space of an autoencoder, thus learning both a nonlinear encoding (lifting) transformation as well as a nonlinear decoding transformation to reconstruct the state.}
elements.sent-2411-046: These works also typically optimize the matrix representation of the operator and a dictionary for lifting simultaneously by stochastic gradient descent without the need to perform a least-squares or pseudoinverse calculation, as in standard EDMD.
elements.sent-2411-047: Relatedly, Constante-Amores, et al. \cite{constante-amores_data-driven_2024, ConstanteAmores2024 applied EDMD-DL to learn a dynamic model for the latent state from an autoencoder-based reduced order model.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Despite these advances, Koopman-based methods with linear time-evolution still fail to provide accurate forecasts of complex systems (see, for example, Ref. \cite{Fox2023, constante-amores_enhancing_2024 ).}
elements.sent-2411-049: In many cases, this is likely due to the fact that certain systems are nonlinearizable \cite{Guckenheimer2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-050: That is, it is not possible to find a finite-dimensional linear representation of a nonlinear system with essentially nonlinear characteristics (such as multiple isolated attracting equilibria) because such systems cannot globally be smoothly conjugate to a finite-dimensional linear system.. \cite{brunton_koopman_2016,kaiser_data-driven_2021,Page2019,cenedese_data-driven_2022,haller_data-driven_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: This is due to the fact that any linear system can have at most one isolated, attracting equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-052: Further discussions on the existence of smooth embeddings of nonlinear systems can be found in the recent works of Kvalheim and Arathoon \cite{arathoon_koopman_2023,kvalheim_linearizability_2024 and Liu et al \cite{liu_non-existence_2023,Liu2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Another recent series of papers have indicated that, from an applied perspective, one mechanism that may be useful for constructing global linearizations is the introduction of discontinuous indicator functions which separate distinct regions of attraction \cite{Bakker2019,Nandanoori2022,pan_lifting_2024 , effectively giving a piecewise linear Koopman representation.}
elements.sent-2411-054: It has also been shown that whether or not a system can be represented linearly in finite dimension is related to the spectrum of the Koopman operator \cite{mezic_spectral_2005, Mezic2020, mezic_koopman_2021 , in that systems where the associated Koopman operator has a continuous spectrum do not admit a finite dimensional linear representation \cite{mezic_koopman_2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-055: This is a substantial limitation of the predictive capability of Koopman-based methods, as it indicates that it is not possible for these methods to fully capture the chaotic dynamics associated with the continuous spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-056: Indeed, many Koopman-based methods for forecasting introduce a nonlinearity into the time-evolution formulation to improve performance, with some doing so explicitly and some subtly.
elements.sent-2411-057: Baddoo et al. \cite{Baddoo2022 proposed learning a nonlinear forcing term jointly with a linear operator using a kernel-based learning strategy to identify a sparse dictionary for the nonlinear forcing.}
elements.sent-2411-058: They show that by disambiguating the linear and nonlinear terms in this way, the identified linear operator is more robust, as it is not corrupted by noise effects from the nonlinear terms as in the standard DMD training procedure.
elements.sent-2411-059: Similarly, Eivazi et al. \cite{Eivazi2021 , introduced a modified version of Hankel-DMD \cite{Arbabi2017, Brunton2017 (a Koopman-based model in time-delay coordinates) that explicitly includes a nonlinear term in the time evolution model, showing that their Koopman-based method provides models which achieve the same level of prediction accuracy as non-Markovian time-series models using recurrent neural networks (RNNs).}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Others have proposed that a way to avoid the issue of the impossibility of conjugacy to a linear system is to avoid including the full state explicitly in the Koopman observables and instead introducing a nonlinearity in decoding the full state from a latent state with linear time evolution.
elements.sent-2411-061: This is the idea behind many Koopman-based autoencoder frameworks \cite{lusch_deep_2018,otto_linearly_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-062: Of particular interest here is a more subtle nonlinearity that has been introduced in several Koopman-based prediction applications, where at each time step in the forward rollout of the observables by the Koopman operator, the observables are projected back to the state space and then ``re-lifted" back to the observable space.
elements.sent-2411-063: This procedure was used in the implementation of EDMD-DL for prediction in the work of Li, et al. \cite{li_extended_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-064: J\"unker et al. \cite{junker_data-driven_2022 also pointed out the effectiveness of this method, showing that this strategy greatly improves the predictive capability of EDMD as compared to purely linear time evolution of the observables for damped pendulum and Duffing oscillator models.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Recently, this procedure was further studied in the work of Constante-Amores et al. \cite{constante-amores_enhancing_2024 in the context of EDMD-DL, where it was shown that this strategy also yields improved performance for several chaotic systems and a comparison was given in terms of the time between these correction steps.}
elements.sent-2411-066: The method was also studied recently by van Goor et al. \cite{van_goor_reprojection_2023 , who showed that by considering the lifting as a mapping to a manifold in a higher dimensional space, the re-lifting procedure can be thought of as a projection back to this manifold, which will not necessarily be invariant under the action of the matrix approximation of the operator.}
elements.sent-2411-067: That is, the re-lifting corrects the EDMD prediction by projecting it back onto the manifold.
elements.sent-2411-068: This interpretation allows for a connection to be made between the projection method and the invariance of the function space spanned by the EDMD dictionary under the action of the Koopman operator.
elements.sent-2411-069: Nehma and Tiwari \cite{Nehma2024a also recently applied this method in their study of learning Koopman representations using Kolmogorov-Arnold networks, showing that this method can be used with a linear quadratic regulator control block.}
elements.sent-2411-070: While these Koopman-based approaches have shown great promise, other data-driven methods have sought to use neural networks to learn dynamics directly on the state space.
elements.sent-2411-071: When the vector field on the right hand side (RHS) of an ODE is represented as a neural network, the resulting model is known as a neural ODE.
elements.sent-2411-072: The neural ODE method was popularized in recent years by Chen et al. \cite{Chen2018 , who observed that gradients with respect to the network parameters can be computed by either backpropagating through the operations of a numerical ODE solver or by an adjoint sensitivity method.}
elements.sent-2411-073: These gradients can then be used to optimize the network parameters by stochastic gradient descent.
elements.sent-2411-074: While neural ODEs have seen a resurgence in recent years, many of the underlying ideas can be found in papers dating back to the 1990s \cite{RicoMartinez1992,RicoMartinez1994,RicoMartinez1993,Chu1991 , such as the work of Rico-Martinez et al. \cite{RicoMartinez1992 , who used a neural ODE to develop a continuous-time model for a chemical reaction from experimental data and showed that the neural ODE model captured bifurcations observed in experiments.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: Another popular approach to developing continuous time models directly on the state space is the sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics (SINDy)\cite{Brunton2016 , which models a vector field on the state space as a sparse linear combination of a predefined dictionary of functions of the state (a.k.a.. features).}
elements.sent-2411-076: SINDy is often presented as more interpretable than neural network-based approaches, as the sparsity-promotion in the optimization attempts to eliminate all but the most significant contributors to the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-077: However, determining an appropriate set of dictionary functions for a given system, is often challenging and may require expert domain knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-078: Additionally, SINDy requires access to data of the time-derivatives of the states during training, whereas neural ODEs only require snapshot data during training.
elements.sent-2411-079: For these reasons, our focus here is on neural ODE models in comparison to EDMD-DL models, as both provide neural network-based modeling strategies to develop models directly from sequences of snapshots.
elements.sent-2411-080: We show here that EDMD-DL with a state space projection also represents the vector field as a linear combination of dictionary elements, where the dictionary is represented by a neural network.
elements.sent-2411-081: In this way, the method can be interpreted as being related to SINDy, without sparsity promotion but on an optimized feature space.
elements.sent-2411-082: In this work, we elucidate the relationship between EDMD-DL with the projection correction step and neural ODEs.
elements.sent-2411-083: Specifically, we point out that by deviating from the typical, linear time evolution of observables in Koopman formulations, the projection step introduces a nonlinearity into the time evolution, which makes the method essentially equivalent to learning a neural network approximation of the flow map, as illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:explanatory .}
elements.sent-2411-084: In this formulation, the flow map approximation has a natural neural network structure that expands out to a high dimensional set of nonlinear features (from EDMD-DL), followed by a linear mapping which selects from those features to represent the flow map.
elements.sent-2411-085: Moreover, EDMD-DL parameterizes the dictionary with neural networks, allowing us to draw parallels to neural ODEs, which represent the vector field of an ODE by a neural network.
elements.sent-2411-086: We show that converting the discrete time EDMD-DL model to a continuous time formulation directly yields a neural ODE, without the need for a separate training procedure.
elements.sent-2411-087: These parallels motivate several model variations developed by combining structures and training methods typically used in neural ODEs and EDMD-DL.
elements.sent-2411-088: In comparing the performance of these methods in numerical examples with chaotic dynamics, we further highlight that by including a projection back to the state space on each step of the EDMD-DL time evolution, the resulting model is essentially equivalent to a neural ODE.
elements.sent-2411-089: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows.. In Sec.
elements.sent-2411-090: We then implement these model variations on two chaotic dynamical systems: the Lorenz system and a nine-mode model for a turbulent shear flow.
elements.sent-2411-091: With these implementations, we assess the performance of the models in terms of short-time prediction error, reconstruction of long-time statistics, and the prediction of rare events.
elements.sent-2411-092: Consider an autonomous dynamical system in continuous time given by an ODE \begin{equation}\frac{d\bx}{dt}=\bff(\bx).\label{eq:ode}\end{equation} where $\bx\in\reals^n$ is the state of the system and denote the associated discrete-time flow map for time interval $\tau$ by $\bF_\tau:\reals^n\to\reals^n$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:flow} \bx(t+\tau) = \bF_\tau(\bx(t)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-093: We can also consider time evolution in a space $\Obs$ of observable functions of the state, $g:\reals^n\to\reals$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Associated with this system is an operator, $\Koop_\tau:\Obs\to\Obs$ defined by \begin{equation}\label{eq:Koopman} \Koop_\tau g(\bx) = g\circ \bfF_\tau (\bx) = g(\bfF_\tau(\bx))\end{equation} which propagates observable functions forward in time along trajectories of the system in \eqref{eq:ode .}
elements.sent-2411-095: This operator $\Koop_\tau$ is known as the Koopman operator, or composition operator \cite{Koopman1931,lasota_chaos_1994 .}
elements.sent-2411-096: The operator $\Koop_\tau$ is linear, as \begin{equation}\begin{split} \Koop_\tau(c_1 g_1 + c_2 g_2) (\bx) = (c_1 g_1 + c_2 g_2)(\bfF_\tau(\bx)) = c_1g_1(\bfF_\tau(\bx)) + c_2g_2(\bfF_\tau(\bx)) = c_1\Koop_\tau g_1(\bx) + c_2\Koop_\tau g_2(\bx) \end{split}\end{equation} for $c_1,c_2\in \reals$ and $g_1,g_2\in \Obs$.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation} \Koop_\tau(c_1 g_1 + c_2 g_2) (\bx) = (c_1 g_1 + c_2 g_2)(\bfF_\tau(\bx)) = c_1g_1(\bfF_\tau(\bx)) + c_2g_2(\bfF_\tau(\bx)) = c_1\Koop_\tau g_1(\bx) + c_2\Koop_\tau g_2(\bx) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-097: The family of operators $\Koop_\tau$ parameterized by $\tau$ has a semigroup structure \cite{lasota_chaos_1994 , and the infinitesimal generator of the Koopman semigroup can be used to express the continuous time form of the dynamics on $\Obs$.}
elements.sent-2411-098: The infinitesimal generator, $\Lie$ is defined by \cite{lasota_chaos_1994 \begin{equation}\label{eq:Lie_op} \Lie g = \lim_{t \to 0^+}\frac{g\circ\bF_t-g}{t} = \lim_{t \to 0^+}\frac{\Koop_\tau g-g}{t} = \lim_{t \to 0^+}\frac{\Koop_\tau - \mathcal{I}}{t}\,g \,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-099: By this definition, the operator $\Lie$ gives the time derivative of $g(\bF_t(\bx))$.
elements.sent-2411-100: Expanding this by chain rule gives the following explicit form. \begin{equation}\Lie g = \frac{d}{dt}g(\bF_t(\bx)) = \frac{\partial g}{\partial \bx}\cdot\frac{d\,\bF_t(\bx)}{dt} = \frac{\partial g}{\partial \bx} \cdot \bff(\bx) \label{eq:Lie}\end{equation} The operator $\Lie$ is commonly referred to as the Koopman generator or the Lie operator \cite{Koopman1931,brunton_modern_2022 , because $\Lie g$ is the Lie derivative of $g$ along the vector field $\bff$ \cite{Abraham2012 .}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Further, by solving \eqref{eq:Lie , the Koopman operator and Lie operator can be shown to be formally related by an exponential \cite{Cvitanovic2016a \begin{equation}\label{eq:Lie_exp} \Koop_\tau = e^{\tau \Lie}\,.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-102: Also, we note that while the Lie operator given in \eqref{eq:Lie represents a linear partial differential equation, this equation is hyperbolic and can be solved by the method of characteristics, wherein each characteristic is determined by solving a nonlinear ODE.}
elements.sent-2411-103: This conceptual point helps to provide some intuition as to how nonlinear dynamics can be represented by a linear operator.
elements.sent-2411-104: Here we briefly review a set of numerical methods for constructing dynamic models from data based on the formalisms introduced in Sec.. \ref{sec:prelim .}
elements.sent-2411-105: Specifically, we will be interested in learning models from a time series dataset, which we will typically organize as snapshot pairs \begin{equation}\label{eq:data} \{(\bx_i,\by_i=\bF_{\tau}(\bx_i))\}_{i=1}^m\end{equation} where $\bx_i\equiv \bx(t_i)$, $\tau=t_{i+1 -t_i$ is the sampling interval, and $m$ is the number of data pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-106: We note that for the continuous time formulations considered below, such as neural ODEs, it is not necessary to have a fixed time interval between datapoints in the dataset, but we will take it to be fixed here for consistency between methods.
elements.sent-2411-107: We will additionally be interested in fitting models using neural networks as function approximators, and those which preserve the Markovian structure assumed in the dynamical systems formulation above -- that future time evolution is determined only from the present states.
elements.sent-2411-108: This requirement excludes several popular deep learning based time series modeling approaches, such as reservoir computing, LSTMs, and transformers.
elements.sent-2411-109: Perhaps the most straightforward way to construct a dynamics model from data is to pose a functional form for the flow map in \eqref{eq:flow , as \begin{equation}\label{eq:disc_time_NN} \bx_{i+1}=\bF_\tau(\bx_i; \theta_\bF)\end{equation} where the parameters $\theta_\bF$ are chosen to minimize the prediction loss \begin{equation}\label{eq:disc_time_objective} J(\theta_\bF) = \sum_{i=1}^m\|\by_i-\bF_\tau(\bx_i; \theta_\bF)\|_2^2\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-110: For example, $\bF_\tau$ can be represented as a simple feedforward neural network and the objective minimized by stochastic gradient descent.
elements.sent-2411-111: Alternatively, $\bF_\tau$ could be assumed to be linear in a predefined set of features $\psi_j(\bx)$, such as monomials, radial basis functions, or a Fourier basis; and then the parameters can be optimized by solving a linear least squares problem.
elements.sent-2411-112: In many cases, we would prefer a continuous time model, which requires an approximation of the ODE vector field $\bff(\bx)$ in \eqref{eq:ode .}
elements.sent-2411-113: When $\bff$ is approximated by a neural network $\bff(\bx; \theta_\bff)$, the resulting model is called a neural ODE \cite{Chen2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-114: The parameters of a neural ODE are optimized to minimize the objective \begin{equation}\label{eq:node_objective} J(\theta_\bff)= \sum_{i=1}^m \left\|\by_i - \hat{\by}_i\right\|_2^2 ,\end{equation} where $\hat{\by _i$ is the prediction of $\by_i$ found by integrating the ODE from $t_i$ to $t_i+\tau$ from initial condition $\bx_i$ \begin{equation}\label{eq:node_solve} \hat{\by}_i = \bx_i + \int_{t_i}^{t_i+\tau}\bff(\bx(t); \theta_\bff) dt\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-115: The objective in \eqref{eq:node_objective is typically optimized by stochastic gradient descent, where the gradients with respect to the parameters $\theta_\bff$ are determined by either solving an adjoint equation backward in time from $t_i+\tau$ to $t_i$ to obtain these sensitivities or by using modern automatic differentiation tools to backpropagate these gradients through the operations of a numerical ODE solver \cite{Chen2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-116: Once the neural ODE model is trained, it can be implemented to predict the time evolution of a system by simply integrating along the learned vector field using a numerical ODE solver from a given initial condition.
elements.sent-2411-117: Extended dynamic mode decomposition (EDMD) \cite{williams_datadriven_2015 is a method for constructing a matrix approximation of the Koopman operator from data by considering the operator acting on elements of a function subspace spanned by a predefined set of dictionary functions.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Consider the dictionary \begin{equation}\DD = \{\psi_j:\reals^n\to\reals\}_{j=1}^k\end{equation} whose elements span a subspace $\Obs_\DD\subset \Obs$.
elements.sent-2411-119: Any observable $g_\bc\in\Obs_\DD$ can be represented as a linear combination of the dictionary elements \begin{equation}\label{eq:gc} g_\bc(\bx) = \sum_{j=1}^k \bc_j \psi_j(\bx) = \mathbf{c}\trans\Psi(\bx)\end{equation} where $\Psi:\reals^n\to\reals^k$ is a vector-valued function containing the elements of $\DD$, and $\mathbf{c}\in\reals^k$ is a vector of projection coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-120: A time-evolved observable $\Koop_\tau g_\bc$ can also be expressed in terms of the dictionary elements as \begin{equation}\Koop_\tau g_\bc(\bx) = \bc\trans \bK_\tau\trans \Psi(\bx) + r_\bc(\bx) \label{eq:koopman_approx}\end{equation} where $\bK_\tau\in \reals^{k\times k}$ is a matrix which approximates the action of the Koopman operator $\Koop_\tau$ by updating the projection coefficients $\bc$ and $r_\bc\in\Obs$ is a residual, which we will seek to minimize by an appropriate choice of $\bK_\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-121: If $\Obs_\DD$ is Koopman invariant, then the operator can be represented exactly as a matrix and the residual, $r_\bc(\bx)=0$.
elements.sent-2411-122: Note that the time-evolved observable can also be represented, using \eqref{eq:Koopman , as \begin{equation}\Koop_\tau g_\bc(\bx) = g_\bc \circ \bF_\tau(\bx) = \bc\trans\Psi \circ \bF_\tau(\bx). \label{eq:koopman_approx_flow}\end{equation} Equating \eqref{eq:koopman_approx and \eqref{eq:koopman_approx_flow , the residual $r_\bc(\bx)$ can be written as \begin{equation}r_\bc(\bx) = \bc\trans\big(\Psi(\bF_\tau(\bx)) - \bK_\tau\trans\Psi(\bx)\big)\,. \label{eq:koopman_approx_resid}\end{equation} Since we would like to minimize the residual for arbitrary observables in $\Obs_\DD$, we can drop the dependence on $\bc$ and seek to minimize the objective \begin{equation}\label{eq:min_K_edmd_sum} J(\bK_\tau) = \sum_{i=1}^m\left\| \Psi(\by_i) - \bK_\tau\trans\Psi(\bx_i)\right\|_2^2\end{equation} where $\by_i = \bx_{i+1 =\bF_\tau(\bx_i)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-123: If the lifted measurement data is organized into the lifted data matrices $\Psi_X,\Psi_Y \in \reals^{k\times m}$ as \begin{equation}\Psi_X = \begin{bmatrix} \Psi(\bx_1) , ~\cdots~ , \Psi(\bx_m) \end{bmatrix} \qquad , \qquad \Psi_Y = \begin{bmatrix} \Psi(\by_1) , ~\cdots~ , \Psi(\by_m) \end{bmatrix}\end{equation} then $J(\bK_\tau)$ can be expressed succinctly as \begin{equation}\label{eq:min_K_EDMD} J(\bK_\tau) = \|\Psi_Y - \bK_\tau\trans\Psi_X \|_F^2\,\end{equation} where $\|\cdot\|_F$ denotes the Frobenius norm.

elements.sent-2411-125: We further detail how such a matrix approximation can be used for predicting the time evolution of the states below in Sec.. \ref{sec:edmd_forecasting .}
elements.sent-2411-126: A challenge of the EDMD approach is finding an appropriate dictionary $\DD$ on which to compute the operator $\bK_t$, as the as the accuracy and convergence of the approximation depend heavily on this choice.
elements.sent-2411-127: Ideally, the subspace spanned by the dictionary should be Koopman invariant so that the residual can be driven to zero.
elements.sent-2411-128: To meet this requirement, it is necessary that the dictionary elements all lie in a space spanned by Koopman eigenfunctions \cite{mezic_spectral_2005, Rowley2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-129: Additionally, we would like a dictionary from which the states can be recovered easily, and preferably linearly, for use in prediction applications.
elements.sent-2411-130: However, finding a dictionary that satisfies these properties is nontrivial, as the appropriate dictionary depends heavily on the underlying dynamical system and many common choices of dictionary do not scale well with the state dimension, making them challenging to apply to systems of dimension greater than 3 or 4.
elements.sent-2411-131: An alternative approach proposed by Li et al. \cite{li_extended_2017 is to allow the vector of dictionary elements $\Psi$ to be represented by a neural network, parameterized by weights $\theta_\Psi$; that is, $\Psi(\bx) = \Psi(\bx; \theta_\Psi)$.}
elements.sent-2411-132: This allows the basis to be optimized jointly along with the matrix approximation of the Koopman operator, $\bK_\tau$, so that the objective in \eqref{eq:min_K_edmd_sum becomes \begin{equation}\label{eq:min_K_EDMD_DL} J(\bK_\tau, \theta_\Psi) = \sum_{i=1}^m\left\|\Psi(\by_i; \theta_\Psi) - \bK_\tau\trans\Psi(\bx_i; \theta_\Psi) \right\|_2^2\end{equation} This approach is known as EDMD with dictionary learning (EDMD-DL).}
elements.sent-2411-133: The original paper of Li et al \cite{li_extended_2017 proposed an iterative procedure for solving \eqref{eq:min_K_EDMD_DL which alternates between two steps in which (1) the dictionary is fixed and the Koopman approximation $\bK_\tau$ is optimized through the pseudoinverse calculation in \eqref{eq:lsq_koopman and then (2) the matrix $\bK_\tau$ is fixed and the dictionary is optimized through stochastic gradient descent.}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: Recently, Constante-Amores et al. showed that these steps can be combined by performing the least-squares calculation for $\bK_\tau$ within the dictionary optimization step and backpropagating through this pseudoinverse-based solution of the least squares problem in optimizing the dictionary \cite{constante-amores_enhancing_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-135: Other works have optimized the elements of $\bK_\tau$ directly along with the dictionary by stochastic gradient descent, avoiding the least squares calculation entirely (e.g., \cite{otto_linearly_2019,lusch_deep_2018,folkestad_koopnet_2022 ).}

elements.sent-2411-146: One common approach to this is to structure the dictionary so that the states $\bx$ are included explicitly \cite{li_extended_2017,constante-amores_enhancing_2024 ; that is, \begin{equation}\label{eq:state_in_dict} \Psi(\bx) = \begin{bmatrix} \bx \tilde{\Psi}(\bx) \end{bmatrix}\end{equation} where $\tilde{\Psi}$ are dictionary elements which are nonlinear functions of $\bx$.}
elements.sent-2411-147: With this structure, the projection back to the state space takes the simple, linear form $\calP\Psi(\bx) = \bP\Psi(\bx)$ with $\bP = \begin{bmatrix \mathbf{I _{n\times n \mathbf{0 _{n\times(k-n) \end{bmatrix}$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-152: As noted by Li, et al \cite{li_extended_2017 , including the states $\bx$ as nontrainable dictionary elements also prevents the training procedure from learning the trivial solution, $\Psi(\bx)=\mathbf{0}$, which is a minimizer of \eqref{eq:min_K_EDMD_DL .}}
elements.sent-2411-153: As pointed out by Otto and Rowley \cite{otto_linearly_2019 , it is also useful to normalize the error in the state prediction and the dictionary prediction in the loss function \begin{equation}\label{eq:edmd_loss} J(\bK_\tau; \theta_\Psi) = \sum_{i=1}^m\left[\frac{\|\by_i - \bP\bK_\tau\trans\Psi(\bx_i;\theta_\Psi) \|_2}{\| \by_i\|_2+\epsilon} + \lambda \, \frac{\|\tilde{\Psi}(\by_i;\theta_\Psi) - \tilde{\bP}\bK_\tau\trans\Psi(\bx_i; \theta_\Psi)\|_2}{\|\tilde{\Psi}(\by_i;\theta_\Psi)\|_2+\epsilon} \right]\end{equation} where $ \tilde{\bP = \begin{bmatrix \mathbf{0 _{(k-n)\times n \mathbf{I _{(k-n)\times (k-n) \end{bmatrix $ is the matrix which extracts only the nonlinear functions $\tilde{\Psi}$ from the dictionary (but not the states $\bx$), $\epsilon$ is a small parameter introduced to avoid divide-by-zero errors, and $\lambda$ is an optional weighting parameter to adjust the relative effect of the two terms.}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-158: A key characteristic of Koopman-based approaches is that, as long as the operator $\calP$ is linear, these methods allow for state trajectories to be predicted from an initial condition using only linear operations (following the initial nonlinear mapping to the feature space).
elements.sent-2411-159: This is key, as preserving linearity is necessary in order to apply linear methods for control \cite{korda_linear_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-160: However, several recent works \cite{junker_data-driven_2022,constante-amores_enhancing_2024,constante-amores_data-driven_2024,van_goor_reprojection_2023 have shown that the predictive capabilities of EDMD models can be substantially improved by performing a projection during the forward rollout where after each step with the Koopman model, the prediction is projected from the lifted state $\Psi(\bx_i)$ back to the original state space, \begin{equation}\label{eq:nonlinprop_psi_i} \bx_{i+1} = \bP\bK_\tau\trans \Psi(\bx_i) \,.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-250: Next we compare these methods on the nine-mode model for a sinusoidal shear flow between parallel plates developed by Moehlis, Faisst, and Eckhardt (MFE) in \cite{Moehlis2004,Moehlis2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-251: This model provides a useful test case, as it displays complex dynamics with many characteristics associated with turbulent shear flows in the transition regime, such as long chaotic transient intervals with rare quasi-laminarization events, with all trajectories eventually collapsing to the laminar state.
elements.sent-2411-252: These complex dynamics allow us to assess not only a model's ability for short time predictions and reproduction of long time statistics in the turbulent region, but also the accuracy of the lifetime predictions and the model's ability to predict extreme events.
elements.sent-2411-253: For these reasons, this model has become a common test-case for data-driven time-series prediction methods, particularly in the fluid dynamics community \cite{Srinivasan2019, Racca2022, Fox2023, constante-amores_data-driven_2024 .}

elements.sent-2411-297: Finally, we assess the ability of the data-driven models to forecast rare quasi-laminarization events within a short time horizon by casting this task as a binary classification problem, following Racca and Magri \cite{Racca2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-298: That is, we evaluate whether extreme events predicted by the model within a specified time interval are true positives ($TP$) or false positives ($FP$).
elements.sent-2411-299: Similarly, when a model does not predict an extreme event in the interval, we evaluate whether this is a true negative ($TN$) or a false negative ($FN$).
elements.sent-2411-300: In this way, we can also evaluate how rapidly the quality of extreme event prediction degrades as the prediction horizon, $h$, before the interval of interest is increased.
elements.sent-2411-301: There are multiple ways to quantify the statistical accuracy of predictions in binary classification problems.
elements.sent-2411-302: Precision quantifies the fraction of true positive predictions out of all positive predictions, $P=TP/(TP+FP)$, while recall quantifies the ratio of true positive predictions to the total number of event occurences, $R=TP/(TP+FN)$.
elements.sent-2411-303: Here we report the $F$-score, which is given by the harmonic mean of precision and recall \begin{equation}F = \frac{2}{P^{-1}+R^{-1}} = \left(1+\frac{FP+FN}{2TP}\right)^{-1}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-304: To compute the $F$-score for our models, we first select a set of starting points from the test dataset evenly spaced at 1 $\tau_L$ apart.

elements.sent-2411-317: Developing and improving upon neural network-based methods for time evolution prediction of dynamical systems is a promising approach for the forecasting of complex dynamical systems.
elements.sent-2411-318: In this work, we have examined the parallels between neural ODE methods and methods based on finite-dimensional approximations of the Koopman operator using neural network-based dictionaries.
elements.sent-2411-319: While methods based on the Koopman operator lead to an approximation of linear dynamics on the space of observables, in practice they often provide poor predictions of the time-evolution of the states due to the difficulty associated with determining a dictionary which spans a Koopman invariant space for a given system.
elements.sent-2411-320: This is especially true for chaotic systems, such as the ones considered here.
elements.sent-2411-321: It has been seen that the state-predictions of these Koopman-based methods can be greatly improved by projecting back to the state space on each timestep.
elements.sent-2411-322: Here we have pointed out that by relifting to the observable space from the state space on each step, we introduce a nonlinearity into the time evolution formulation, resulting in a nonlinear discrete-time map on the state space.
elements.sent-2411-323: Therefore using an EDMD approximation of the operator in this way with a neural network dictionary can be seen as a discrete-time version of a neural ODE.
elements.sent-2411-324: Furthermore, we can also apply a similar projection strategy to the continuous time formulations of EDMD based on the Koopman generator to obtain a continuous time neural ODE directly from the EDMD-DL-optimized operator and dictionary without any further training.
elements.sent-2411-325: Additionally, the model structures arising from EDMD-DL with projection naturally possess a neural network structure that is commonly used in neural ODEs, in which the state is first expanded out to a high-dimensional feature vector (represented by a multi-layer neural network) followed by a linear mapping back to the state space to yield the vector field of the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-326: Inspired by these observations, we have implemented several variations of neural ODEs and EDMD-DL, developed by combining different aspects of their respective model structures and training procedures.
elements.sent-2411-327: We provide performance comparisons on two chaotic systems to demonstrate the equivalence of these methods and highlight the aspects of each model that lead to improved performance.
elements.sent-2411-328: Specifically, we show that the novel neural ODE architecture based on the dictionary structure typically used in EDMD-DL, where the state is explicitly separated from the nonlinear dictionary elements, leads to a small improvement in the short-time prediction performance of neural ODEs, as compared to the standard neural ODE architecture.
elements.sent-2411-329: In the context of neural ODEs, separating the linear and nonlinear parts of the vector field provides a nice structure, as the neural network only has to account for the nonlinear dynamics, with the linear terms represented separately.
elements.sent-2411-330: In the examples considered here, the EDMD-structured neural ODE architecture, trained with the usual neural ODE loss, performed best overall, suggesting that this should be the standard structure for neural ODEs.
elements.sent-2411-331: We see that each of the neural ODE methods and EDMD-DL methods with projection tend to perform comparably on these systems in terms of short-time trajectory prediction, reconstruction of long-time statistics, and the prediction of extreme events; with all of them offering a significant improvement over the standard EDMD-DL linear time evolution on the space of observables.
elements.sent-2411-332: Furthermore, we emphasize that each of these models perform very well in terms of short-time prediction, with less than $3\%$ relative error at $t=\tau_L$ for each of the chaotic systems considered here and rare event prediction capability comparable to the non-Markovian approach of Racca and Magri \cite{Racca2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-333: In addition to accurate short-time tracking, we have also demonstrated that the model predictions are statistically accurate for long times as well, as they yield accurate reconstructions of the long-time probability density functions on the state space and accurately predict the turbulent lifetime for the MFE model of a turbulent shear flow.
elements.sent-2411-334: These findings highlight the promise of neural ODEs and Koopman-based approaches for developing data-driven models capable of generating accurate time evolution predictions in complex and chaotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-335: This work was supported by the Office of Naval Research (grant No. N00014-18-1-2865 (Vannevar Bush Faculty Fellowship)).
elements.sent-2411-336: These results highlight the equivalence of the EDMD-DL implementation with a state space projection to a neural ODE representation of the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-337: We show that this step converts the linear dynamics on the space of observables to a nonlinear system on the state space, rendering the method equivalent to a neural ODE of a particular form.
elements.sent-2411-338: This insight helps to form new connections between these seemingly distinct approaches and inspires new methods that combine the strengths of each.

elements.sent-2411-339: Forecasting the time-evolution of complex dynamical systems from data is of great importance to many scientific and engineering applications, particularly as data availability has increased in recent years.
elements.sent-2411-340: This demand has driven the development of neural network-based models for time-series prediction.
elements.sent-2411-341: In this work, we establish a connection between two popular approaches to data-driven modeling of dynamical systems: neural ODEs \cite{Chen2018 , which represent the dynamics directly on the state space, and extended dynamic mode decomposition with dictionary learning (EDMD-DL) \cite{li_extended_2017 , which models the dynamics on the space of observable functions using a finite-dimensional approximation of the Koopman operator.}}
elements.sent-2411-342: Specifically, we show that implementations of EDMD-DL which include a state space projection at each timestep are equivalent to neural ODEs, as this results in a nonlinear neural network-based representation of the dynamical system on the state space.
elements.sent-2411-343: This contrasts with the standard Koopman-based approach, which yields a linear representation of the dynamics on the space of observable functions.
elements.sent-2411-344: The equivalence of the projected Koopman formulation to a neural ODE is illustrated through numerical examples on chaotic systems, where the projected Koopman method achieves predictive performance equivalent to that of a neural ODE while significantly outperforming the standard linear Koopman approach.
elements.sent-2411-345: The equivalence between these methods in discrete time is depicted schematically in Fig. \ref{fig:explanatory .}
elements.sent-2411-346: Section \ref{sec:continuous_edmd details this equivalence in continuous time.}
elements.sent-2411-347: Koopman operator methods aim to provide linear representations of nonlinear dynamical systems by describing time evolution of observable functions \cite{budisic_applied_2012,mezic_analysis_2013,brunton_modern_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-348: In the Koopman formalism, the dynamics are described by a linear operator, the Koopman operator, which typically acts on an infinite dimensional function space.
elements.sent-2411-349: Such approximations hold great promise for many application areas, as they enable the use of modal analysis to identify structure in nonlinear systems \cite{mezic_spectral_2005,mezic_analysis_2013 , they can allow for dimension reduction when dealing with high-dimensional data \cite{rowley_model_2017 , and they can potentially be paired with linear methods for forecasting, estimation, and control \cite{otto_koopman_2021 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-350: One of the earliest and most common methods to approximate the Koopman operator is dynamic mode decomposition (DMD) \cite{Schmid2010,Rowley2009 , which determines a best-fit linear time-evolution operator given time series data from the system.}
elements.sent-2411-351: However, the finite dimensional approximation obtained from DMD can only be rigorously connected to the Koopman operator if the observable functions used in the computation lie within an invariant subspace spanned by eigenfunctions of the Koopman operator \cite{mezic_spectral_2005,Rowley2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-352: In practice, however, this is usually not the case, and the lack of an invariant subspace leads to closure issues in approximating the operator.
elements.sent-2411-353: Extended dynamic mode decomposition (EDMD) \cite{williams_datadriven_2015 , performs the DMD computation on measurement data that is lifted to a higher dimensional space by evaluating a pre-defined set of dictionary functions on the data.}
elements.sent-2411-354: EDMD with dictionary learning (EDMD-DL)\cite{li_extended_2017 represents the dictionary functions as a neural network and optimizes both the operator matrix and the dictionary to minimize a prediction loss.}
elements.sent-2411-355: Despite these advances, Koopman-based methods with linear time-evolution still fail to provide accurate forecasts of complex systems (see, for example, Refs.
elements.sent-2411-356: Indeed, many Koopman-based methods for forecasting introduce a nonlinearity into the time-evolution formulation to improve performance \cite{li_extended_2017,lusch_deep_2018,otto_linearly_2019,Baddoo2022,Eivazi2021 , with some doing so explicitly and some subtly.}
elements.sent-2411-357: Of particular interest here is a nonlinearity that has been introduced in several Koopman-based prediction applications, where at each time step in the forward rollout of the observables by the Koopman operator, the observables are projected back to the state space and then ``re-lifted" back to the observable space.

elements.sent-2411-367: In this work, we elucidate the relationship between EDMD-DL with the projection step and neural ODEs.
elements.sent-2411-368: Specifically, we point out that by deviating from the typical, linear time evolution of observables in Koopman formulations, the projection step introduces a nonlinearity into the time evolution, which makes the method equivalent to learning a neural network approximation of the flow map, as illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:explanatory .}
elements.sent-2411-369: Moreover, EDMD-DL parameterizes the dictionary with neural networks, allowing us to draw parallels to neural ODEs.
elements.sent-2411-370: In comparing the performance of these methods in numerical examples with chaotic dynamics, we further highlight that by including a projection back to the state space on each step of the EDMD-DL time evolution, the resulting model is equivalent to a neural ODE.
elements.sent-2411-371: These results clarify an important point regarding the implementation of Koopman operator approximations for time-series prediction --- namely, that a Koopman-based predictor with a state-space projection step provides a representation of the nonlinear discrete-time flow map on the state space and does not preserve the linear time-evolution that is typically associated with Koopman-based methods.

elements.sent-2411-389: Continuous-time and discrete-time models each have their own advantages and disadvantages.
elements.sent-2411-390: In many cases, especially when dealing with physical systems governed by continuous-time equations of motion, a continuous-time model is preferred as it more naturally represents continuous-time physics.
elements.sent-2411-391: Additionally, continuous-time models offer greater versatility in terms of temporal spacing of the training data and predictions, whereas discrete-time models are typically constrained to a single fixed timestep.
elements.sent-2411-392: It has also been shown that neural ODE methods tend to outperform discrete-time models in cases where the data is widely spaced in time \cite{Linot2022 , which is the case in many real-world data scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-393: However, in applications where model runtime is critical and predictions are needed only at a constant sampling frequency, discrete-time models can be advantageous.
elements.sent-2411-394: These models often provide faster inference since they generally require fewer function evaluations per timestep than neural ODEs, which typically use more sophisticated timestepping schemes.

elements.sent-2411-398: Extended dynamic mode decomposition (EDMD) \cite{williams_datadriven_2015 constructs a matrix approximation of the Koopman operator, $\bK_\tau$ by solving a least squares problem to minimize the objective \begin{equation}\label{eq:min_K_edmd_sum} J(\bK_\tau) = \sum_{i=1}^m\left\| \Psi(\by_i) - \bK_\tau\trans\Psi(\bx_i)\right\|_2^2\end{equation} over the dataset of interest for a given vector of dictionary functions, $\Psi$.}

elements.sent-2411-455: The ergodic average of the extreme event indicator function for each of the models is computed over the trajectory datasets used in Fig. \ref{fig:MFE_longstats , which consist of simulations from each model from 100 initial conditions for 30,000 timesteps or until relaminarization is predicted.}

elements.sent-2411-457: This result further demonstrates that EDMD-DL with projection and each of the neural ODE models perform comparably, yielding an ergodic average of this indicator that is close to the value when calculated on the test data.
elements.sent-2411-458: This indicates that these models successfully capture the long-time statistics of extreme events.
elements.sent-2411-459: While methods based on the Koopman operator typically lead to an approximation of linear dynamics on the space of observables, in practice these linear methods often provide poor predictions of the time-evolution of the states due to the difficulty associated with determining a dictionary which spans a Koopman invariant space for a given system.
elements.sent-2411-460: Here we have pointed out that by relifting to the observable space from the state space on each step, a nonlinearity is introduced into the EDMD time evolution formulation, resulting in a nonlinear discrete-time map on the state space.
elements.sent-2411-461: While including the state space projection step in the EDMD-DL timestepping procedure does reduce the model to a neural network representation of the dynamics on the state space, the training procedure and structure of the model are the same as in standard EDMD-DL.
elements.sent-2411-462: Therefore, the model inherits the benefits of the EDMD-DL method.
elements.sent-2411-463: It provides a matrix approximation of the Koopman operator, which can be used to study Koopman eigenvalues, eigenfunctions, and modes \cite{williams_datadriven_2015,li_extended_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-464: This has been demonstrated in the original EDMD-DL paper \cite{li_extended_2017 , where it was also shown that optimizing the basis in EDMD-DL typically yields more accurate and efficient representations of the operator and its spectrum than alternative choices of basis, such as radial basis functions or Hermite polynomials.}
elements.sent-2411-465: Many previous works have shown that analysis of Koopman eigenfunctions can be used to glean physical insights into the structure underlying a dynamical system by identifying large scale structures and their associated frequencies in high-dimensional systems, parameterizing basins of attraction, and identifying invariant sets and coherent sets (see, for example \onlinecite{budisic_applied_2012,mezic_analysis_2013,rowley_model_2017,brunton_modern_2022 for detailed reviews of these applications).}
elements.sent-2411-466: In this work, we have implemented several variations of neural ODEs and EDMD-DL, developed by combining different aspects of their respective model structures and training procedures.
elements.sent-2411-467: The essentially equivalent performance between EDMD-DL with projection and the neural ODE models, in contrast to the failure of linear EDMD-DL, further emphasizes our main point: the state space projection makes the EDMD-DL model equivalent to a neural ODE in that it gives a nonlinear, neural network representation of the dynamics on the state space.
elements.sent-2411-468: We also show that the novel EDMD-structured neural ODE architecture yields a small improvement in the short-time prediction performance of neural ODEs, as compared to the standard neural ODE architecture.
elements.sent-2411-469: In the examples considered here, the EDMD-structured neural ODE architecture, trained with the usual neural ODE loss, performed best overall, suggesting that this structure can be beneficial to the performance of neural ODEs on chaotic systems such as these.
elements.sent-2411-470: Furthermore, each of these models performs very well in terms of short-time prediction, with less than $3\%$ relative error at $t=\tau_L$ for each of the chaotic systems considered here and rare event prediction capability comparable to the non-Markovian approach of Racca and Magri \cite{Racca2022 .}
